title: Lesson 258
transcript: In this module, we're going to talk about the importance of applying and enforcing secure coding guidelines and standards within the organization. It's really important for developers and the development environment to use the latest standards and guidelines that ensure the development of secure applications and what we call this is secure coding of course and so it's important for developers and the analysts and others that work in your development environments to understand what secure coding actually is. And the industry has really come up with frameworks and other tools that allow organizations to employ what we call secure coding guidelines. That means the developers and others at work in this environment are aware of some of the most common vulnerabilities and exploits that are typically available through applications. And so if we develop applications and design them in such a way to address these in the first place, then overall the applications that we're ultimately using in the organization are going to be more secure. But that means developers and analysts need to understand some of the exploits that you see here up on your screen. So for example, covert channels. I know we've talked about that before. But the way that we develop the functionality and intelligence in applications should prevent any sort of covert channels from existing. And of course you remember that there are two types of covert channels. storage and timing and anything related to applications would probably involve storage cover channels, but if we develop and design applications to address those. It's a better way to address security overall. We also need to address something that we've had a problem with ever since we started to develop applications. and that is what we call buffer overflows. And the idea of buffer flows, we're gonna talk about in the coming slides, but basically when you look at what applications do, they take input, we process it and we provide output. But when we do that, we have to design the application in such a way so that it uses storage in order to be able to take that input and process it and provide output. And so we have to allocate certain storage for data and other things to be handled within the application itself and its logic. We call those buffers. The problem is we have to set up the buffer to be the perfect size. But then once we're using those applications, if somehow that application is subjected to much more information than the buffer was set up to handle, then we may have what's called a buffer overflow condition. And if you look at the history of applications, it's not uncommon for us to see dozens of new buffer overflow attacks that appear in the span of just a few weeks. And unfortunately the only way to address this is to patch the application for known buffer overflow attacks. But by doing that you may be addressing the ones that we know of, but you may be introducing a whole bunch of new ones that we don't know about. And so buffer overflows is a problem that we've had for decades ever since we started to develop applications. And unfortunately, it's not really a proactive approach to how we prevent them. We have to keep applying patches and changing the logic. and it would be nice to have dynamic buffers that we can use in applications but that's just not something that we can use and so we have to constantly apply new logic to be able to prevent all of those as you'll see. Some of the other issues that secure coding really needs to address are what we call object reuse and if you remember back to when we discussed the concept of object reuse it simply means the ability to overwrite storage where we've written for example secure and sensitive information. The idea is most applications don't have the ability to overwrite storage where we've kept sensitive information. it would be nice to be able to do that automatically. I don't know any operating system or application that has this functionality built in where once we write some sensitive information to an area of storage, we are able to overwrite it automatically so that further subjects that come along and use that same storage would not be able to interpret that sensitive information that we may have left there. So object reuse is the ability to overwrite sensitive information that we've left in certain storage areas within the application itself. We also have what's called executable mobile code. And if you think about those words, mobile code is simply code that moves. This typically happens when you click on a link on a website, for example. And what happens at that point is that the code that needs to be run is downloaded to your machine and that's where it's executed. The problem obviously with this is that that code may be malicious and so it would be nice to be able to look at that code before we let it run in our production environments and so one of the ways that we can address executable mobile code is to test it in what we call sandbox environments and you'll remember what a sandbox environment actually is it's simply a safe play area that we establish as part of that architecture where we can test certain things to make sure that they are not malicious. So anything that is mobile, executable code that we don't know whether it's malicious or not we would want to test in a sandbox environment before we let it loose in our production environments. The next item that you see on your list there is something that we've described in a different module. It's called TALK2 and those letters really stand for time of check versus time of use. It's when control information is used to make security decisions and control information is useful in things like access control and to enforce other security requirements. But the idea of this is if those parameters change, between the time that we actually look at them and when they're actually enforced. If there's a delay between those two periods, it may lead to problems related to exploits and vulnerabilities. And I think one of the examples that we always use to describe what this is all about is if you come in in the morning and you log into your network At that point is when you're given access to network services. But let's say the network administrator, you know, an hour later deletes your user ID. When is it that that system is going to make sure that you're still a valid user on that network? Well, it's the next time you log in. but as far as the network is concerned you still have privileges across the network but you shouldn't because your user ID has been deleted and so that's a problem related to time of check versus time of use and that's just one example of where this might happen where again control information used in order to enforce security rules but there's a delay between the time that we look at those parameters and the time that we actually enforce them. The next item that you see there is back doors, also referred to as trap doors, sometimes also referred to as maintenance hooks. And these typically are put in by developers to make their jobs easier, especially through the development process. so that they can go into an application and fix things as they need or to troubleshoot very quickly. So these are legitimate ways for developers to handle problems but typically during the development process. The problem is when these applications are put into production and those back doors and trap doors still exist. If people figure out how to use those back doors to get into the application then they may be able to bypass your normal access controls that everybody should be subjected to. And we've seen all kinds of examples of back doors and trap doors that have existed in not only operating systems but also systems and applications where you might press a certain key sequence and you're into some portion of that architecture that you shouldn't be able to get into. That's an example of back doors and trap doors. Problem is when we buy software we are typically not given the source code to be able to identify whether any back doors or trap doors actually exist. And so we're really relying on the vendors to provide code and applications that are free of back doors and trap doors. The next one. And again, these are just examples of what developers and designers in our programming environments need to be able to address as what we call secure coding guidelines. Typically these are vulnerabilities that appear and exist in every application, every system, so we need to address them. as easily as possible or as good as possible. Malformed input simply means not doing a good enough job of data and input validation because we don't always think of all of the different ways that we need to validate input. And if you look at the most common attacks that exist out there, especially with web applications like cross-site scripting, SQL injection, cross-site request forgery. You know, they're all really due to one problem and that is inadequate data validation or inadequate input validation. so it's pretty important that we think about those requirements when we're designing and building the applications so that we don't have to address them later but as you know it's pretty difficult to think of all of the different ways that attackers can modify our input or inject certain things in our input that is already trusted by application and that's what leads to a lot of the exploits and vulnerabilities that we experience today. The last one that you see there is a term that the industry has been using for the last number of years. and it's called citizen developers and what it really means is you know all of us today have some powerful tools available to us that really make us developers and the ability to use some of these tools is very dangerous unless we are given the right levels of education and awareness and understanding of how those tools can be misused and can be used to provide exploits and vulnerabilities. perfect example is SQL I mean these are tools that we typically give right to the users to be able to allow them to query and interface with their own databases but when you look at some of these very powerful tools like SQL A lot of the people using those tools are not aware of secure programming and what some of these commands may lead to from an exploit or a vulnerability perspective. And so what security needs to focus on is providing the right levels of awareness and education to the people that we call citizen developers, normal users that have access to very powerful tools that provide opportunities for attackers. and that's what we call citizen developers. Now, we did talk about something called buffer overflows. This is one of the biggest problems that we've ever had. I mean, ever since we started to develop applications decades ago, we've had this problem of buffer overflows and as I mentioned earlier buffer overflows simply means we have to establish a size for the buffers that are used really to allow input and process that input which is basically what's happening any application the problem is if we've set up a buffer to be this big but somehow when that application is being used it is subjected to this much information how does the application handle the overflow amount is or could be a problem and and if that attack has been done creatively it may allow the attackers to put that overflow amount in an area where executions are run according to that system which may allow them to elevate their privilege levels And so we've got an animation here that really describes what this problem looks like. So if you look at Program B instructions and data, that's what we've allocated in storage for Program B. But Program A has been influenced, for example, by the attacker. And what they've done is they've subjected program A to much more information than its buffer, for example, is built and capable of handling. That means the yellow area that you see on your screen there is the overflow amount. And if the overflow amount That includes executions, like clever executions that the attacker has put in to overflow into program B that may allow that attacker to elevate their privilege levels and be able to do things that they shouldn't. So that's what we call a buffer overflow condition. And like we said before, happens a lot. We've seen all kinds of examples. It's one of the biggest problems that we've ever seen related to applications and secure coding that we need to address. The problem is You know improper bounce checking is what really causes buffer overflow conditions, so the proper fix for this is is to properly implement and enforce bounce checking but that's easier said than done and so when you look at the guidance that exists out there for preventing buffer overflows it's what you see here on this slide so doing proper bounce checking improving your software development process so that the developers and the designers are actually aware of not only the ones that we know of but possible ones that could result in how we've set up how buffers are being used within our applications. you know checking for example and detecting you know things that are happening in your runtime environments may allow us to react before these things become problem And of course, again, educating the developers so that we're always using the latest tools that maybe have some capabilities built in to prevent buffer overflows is really how we address this from an industry perspective. Now one of the other really important things that we need to address today in our development environments is the use of APIs. And you'll know what API stands for, Application Programming Interfaces. What we create today are complex environments that require a whole bunch of different components of applications to talk to each other. And the way that typically these components talk to each other is through what we call APIs. Think of APIs as the go between the translators that really allow different components of applications to be able to talk to each other. So APIs will pass a lot of data between components. And what you see on your screen here are the two most commonly used API formats and protocols that exist out there, REST and SOAP. They are typically different. and companies will choose, for example, what may make more sense for them to use. But the difference here is that Rest is more lightweight, whereas Soap has more security capabilities built in. But regardless, as far as the exam is concerned, you don't have to go and learn everything about rest and soap but just understand that there are a lot of vulnerabilities associated with each and if you're going to use these as your API formats for example in your web environments it's pretty important for the people that are designing and developing how those different components talk to each other to understand the vulnerabilities that exist with these and address those in how we implement. Implementation of these is always what dictates whether they become secure or not. And if you look at the industry, there is guidance that is provided in how we protect any API format including REST and SOAP. And what you see here are some of the guidance that people out there, the experts, will give us, you know, do proper authentication and identification of any tokens that are passed by using those APIs. We use encryption to protect the confidentiality of very sensitive information. We do adequate data validation as much as possible so that we prevent injection and other problems related to input. We use gateways that include applications that can look at what's being passed and enforce security rules. enforce quotas and throttling so that attackers cannot impact the volume and anything else that's being passed. And of course, just like anything else, especially related to applications, it's pretty important that we do the proper level of testing and validation to ensure that our applications and APIs that are talking to different components are doing it in the best way possible which means the most efficient way possible but also the most secure way possible. And so here is what as a summary we can provide as secure coding practices. I mean this is really a summary of what guidance that is provided out there really tells us to be able to address in any development environment so that ultimately your applications are as secure as they can be. We do adequate data validation or input validation. We do good authentication and password management. We do good session management. We use cryptography to address the five services that it can. We handle errors properly so that it doesn't provide any problems related to security. We configure the way that APIs and other components talk to each other to provide the ultimate security capabilities. We protect not only the application but all of the other components that make up the entire architecture. For example, the database and the data and the hardware and the operating system. And of course, one of the last things that they talk about is how we also need to do secure memory management, how memory is used within the applications and the systems. need to be done in the most secure way possible. So this is a summary of some of the things that your developers and designers need to be aware in what we call secure coding guidelines and practices. In other words your developers need to also be very aware and knowledgeable in security these days for organizations to produce and use secure applications and systems.
